claims.statement: aws-cli's upstream is the canonical AWS-owned repository github.com/aws/aws-cli.
claims.qualification: Repository identity; the canonical machine-readable record is data/tool.json.

claims.statement: aws-cli is implemented in Python.
claims.qualification: The pinned checkout declares requires-python >= 3.9.

claims.statement: aws-cli is distributed under the Apache-2.0 license.
claims.qualification: License text is in the pinned checkout.

claims.statement: The smoke image reports aws-cli version 2.36.1 in-container.
claims.qualification: The inherited page recorded the version as unknown; the receipted in-container self-report is 2.36.1.

claims.statement: The reviewed source is tag 2.36.1 at commit 12d962d239b9fd0669951c4d27dc366388abba2d.
claims.qualification: The commit SHA is recorded in the groundwork report; the version-help receipt records the version string but not the git SHA.

claims.statement: aws-cli is assigned study Tier 1 as a primary comparison baseline.
claims.qualification: A study-design tier assignment is not a testable property of the tool.

evidence.reason: Tier assignment is a study-design decision, not a testable observation about aws-cli.

claims.statement: aws-cli's testability is trivial: no patch or fork was required to exercise it.
claims.qualification: Supported by its running unmodified in the official upstream image during groundwork, anonymously and with no self-authored Dockerfile. The receipted run confirms unmodified official-image execution; the testability judgment itself is supported by that observation, not independently confirmed.

claims.statement: Both s3 ls and s3api list-objects-v2 list through a paginated ListObjectsV2 continuation-token chain.
claims.qualification: Source establishes both paginator paths; a single --debug probe of one s3api invocation observed the chain. Every-mode verifier PASS proves completeness, not concurrency.

claims.statement: A --debug probe of one s3api list-objects-v2 invocation issued serial single-MainThread requests, each carrying the prior response's continuation token.
claims.qualification: This is one s3api invocation, not an exhaustive per-surface or per-flag sweep.

claims.statement: s3 ls consumes its paginator in a synchronous loop with no threads, so it lists serially.
claims.qualification: Source-established; the s3 ls surface was not separately debug-probed.

claims.statement: S3 caps a single listing page at 1,000 keys, the server-side MaxKeys limit.
claims.qualification: The 1,000-key cap is the S3 ListObjectsV2 server limit; --page-size is an upper bound the server will not exceed.

claims.statement: The --debug probe issued three ListObjectsV2 requests to list a 2,549-key prefix, consistent with the 1,000-key page cap.
claims.qualification: One observed probe; the request count ceil(2549/1000)=3 is consistent with the server cap but is not an exhaustive page-size sweep.

claims.statement: Neither s3 ls nor s3api has any listing parallelism; the only concurrency in aws-cli is in transfers such as s3 cp and s3 sync.
claims.qualification: Source-primary across both surfaces; the single-thread observation covers one probed s3api invocation, and 'regardless of flags' is a source extrapolation, not an exhaustive smoke.

claims.statement: Listing one billion objects would require roughly one million sequential ListObjectsV2 round trips.
claims.qualification: Arithmetic extrapolation from the 1,000-key page cap; no billion-object run was performed.

evidence.reason: The arithmetic derivation was not run at scale.

claims.statement: aws-cli emits the continuation token only to stdout and persists nothing, so a killed unbounded run leaves no token to resume from.
claims.qualification: Source-established; killing a process cannot demonstrate the absence of saved state, so this half was not probed.

claims.statement: The --max-items to NextToken to --starting-token resume primitive round-tripped cleanly, returning 1,000 plus 1,549 = 2,549 distinct keys with zero duplicates and no gap.
claims.qualification: This is deliberate chunked continuation; no process was killed, so it is not a crash-resume test.

claims.statement: AWS guidance redirects large-scale listing away from a live LIST toward S3 Inventory.
claims.qualification: The Inventory redirect is an AWS re:Post knowledge-base article; the CLI user guide covers only the narrower pagination-knob point.

claims.statement: s3 ls --recursive listed the full 148,917-key bucket and passed the verifier.
claims.qualification: One anonymous smoke run against noaa-normals-pds; a completeness verdict, not a benchmark result.

claims.statement: s3api list-objects-v2 --output text listed the full 148,917-key bucket and three scoped prefixes, all verifier PASS.
claims.qualification: Anonymous smoke runs against noaa-normals-pds; completeness verdicts, not benchmark results.

claims.statement: The --page-size flag maps to PaginationConfig.PageSize and is sent to the server as MaxKeys.
claims.qualification: It is an upper bound on keys per request, not a guarantee, and the server caps it at 1,000.

claims.statement: Whether page sizes above 1,000 have any effect, and whether sub-1,000 sizes only add requests, was not tested.
claims.qualification: No page-size sweep was run; flagged for the benchmark phase.

evidence.reason: No above-1000 or sub-1000 page-size sweep was run.

claims.statement: s3api exposes --starting-token and --max-items, while s3 ls exposes neither and offers only --page-size.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the flag tables and the s3 ls help capture confirms its narrower argument set.

claims.statement: The --output flag supports json (default), text, table, yaml, yaml-stream, and off, more members than the inherited json/text/table/yaml list.
claims.qualification: The inherited list omitted yaml-stream and off.

claims.statement: Every smoke mode ran anonymously via --no-sign-request, a global flag that sets botocore signature to UNSIGNED for both s3 and s3api, with zero credentials supplied.
claims.qualification: A --debug probe observed auth_type 'none' and zero Authorization headers; the flag applies to both surfaces.

claims.statement: aws-cli's --cli-read-timeout and --cli-connect-timeout default to 60 seconds, and botocore retries default to max_attempts=3.
claims.qualification: Defaults read from source; a value of 0 disables the timeout, and aws-cli itself adds no --max-attempts flag.

claims.statement: How the timeouts and retries behave under fault injection was not tested.
claims.qualification: No fault injection was performed at smoke; deferred to the replay-server phase.

evidence.reason: No fault injection was performed at smoke.

claims.statement: A manual four-shard-plus-remainder prefix fan-out reconstructed the full 148,917-key bucket with zero cross-shard duplicates and zero missing or extra keys.
claims.qualification: Fan-out is caller-owned; aws-cli has no internal listing concurrency, and the shards were run serially.

claims.statement: A secondhand survey reports aws-cli listing 15 million objects in 1110 seconds, about 13,500 objects per second, from the PS3 author's own comparison table.
claims.qualification: Smoke makes no comparative timing claim; this inherited secondhand figure has not been reproduced.

evidence.reason: No comparative benchmark has reproduced this secondhand throughput figure.

claims.statement: A third-party report, aws/aws-cli issue 1118, describes a roughly 12-million-object listing running one to two hours and then failing mid-run.
claims.qualification: A third-party report at scale and fault-injection scope, not reproduced; the exact invocation must be reproduced before it ships as a finding.

evidence.reason: The third-party mid-run failure was not reproduced.

claims.statement: One billion objects at roughly one million calls and about 30 ms round-trip time gives an approximately eight-hour best-case listing.
claims.qualification: Arithmetic derivation from the page cap and an assumed round-trip time; not run at scale.

evidence.reason: The eight-hour estimate was not measured at scale.

claims.statement: aws-cli is officially distributed as an image and installers for both amd64 and arm64.
claims.qualification: The native arm64 run is receipted; amd64 availability rests on the upstream distribution matrix, not a groundwork run. The inherited 'no install friction' judgment was not evidence-recorded and is dropped. The amd64 distribution facet is documentation-supported by the preserved architecture matrix in research/report.md (upstream Dockerfile uses the x86_64 installer zip; the aarch64 zip exists).

claims.statement: aws-cli is simple, well-documented, and predictable in its output formats at the surface, but its memory model is not evident from surface documentation.
claims.qualification: The caveat is the buffering-versus-streaming memory split recorded as claim memory-format-split.

claims.statement: Listing memory behavior splits by output format: s3 ls, s3api --output text, and s3api --output yaml-stream stream page-by-page and hold no key set, while s3api --output json (default), --output yaml, and --output table buffer the entire result via build_full_result().
claims.qualification: Corrects the 'nothing hidden' surface claim and an earlier draft and merge-commit that placed text among the buffering formats. Whether the buffered path OOMs at scale is not settled by smoke.

claims.statement: s3 ls s3://bucket/ without --recursive lists only the first delimiter level, showing folders as PRE rollups rather than the whole bucket.
claims.qualification: Corrects the 'nothing hidden' claim; a common footgun, source-established and confirmed by the delimiter-root smoke run.

claims.statement: aws-cli has no Parquet output format.
claims.qualification: The 'no Parquet' capability gap holds; the output choices are json, text, table, yaml, yaml-stream, and off.
